proposal_text: This project proposes to install camera-based emotion recognition systems in six secondary school classrooms in Dublin to study the relationship between student emotional engagement and learning outcomes. The system will use computer vision to classify student facial expressions into six emotional categories (happy, sad, angry, surprised, fearful, neutral) at 5-second intervals throughout lessons. Data will be collected over one academic term covering approximately 180 students aged 13-17. Parental consent will be obtained but students will not be given an individual opt-out mechanism that allows them to remain in the classroom without being monitored. The emotion recognition system has been validated primarily on adult Western faces and has not been tested for accuracy on adolescent faces or faces from diverse ethnic backgrounds. Aggregated emotional engagement scores will be shared with teachers to inform pedagogical adjustments.

assessment.risk_summary: The research involves high-risk biometric data collection on minors without individual opt-out mechanisms, raising significant ethical concerns around privacy, psychological well-being, and algorithmic bias. The unvalidated emotion recognition system's potential inaccuracy for adolescents and diverse ethnic groups introduces risks of misclassification and discriminatory outcomes. Continuous surveillance in educational settings may undermine student autonomy and trust.

identified_risks.risk: Biometric surveillance of minors without individual opt-out options
identified_risks.explanation: Students cannot opt out of monitoring despite parental consent, violating autonomy and privacy rights

identified_risks.risk: Algorithmic bias due to unvalidated model performance on adolescent faces and diverse ethnic groups
identified_risks.explanation: System accuracy has not been tested on target demographic, risking misclassification and discriminatory outcomes

identified_risks.risk: Psychological harm from constant emotional surveillance
identified_risks.explanation: Continuous monitoring may cause anxiety or self-censorship among students

identified_risks.risk: Data protection risks from biometric dataset collection
identified_risks.explanation: Facial expression data constitutes sensitive personal information requiring strict safeguards

applicable_requirements.requirement_text: Research must contribute to society and human well-being — discuss how applications may harm individuals, groups or society and ways to mitigate such harms
applicable_requirements.action_needed: Conduct comprehensive harm assessment with mitigation strategies for vulnerable adolescents

applicable_requirements.requirement_text: Assess and communicate degree to which datasets are representative of intended population — claims of diverse representation must be substantiated
applicable_requirements.action_needed: Validate model accuracy across adolescent demographics before deployment

applicable_requirements.requirement_text: Assess whether organisation is obligated to conduct a FRIA (deployer of high-risk AI system)
applicable_requirements.action_needed: Complete Formal Risk Impact Assessment for biometric surveillance in educational context

applicable_requirements.requirement_text: AI system must not subordinate, coerce, deceive, manipulate, objectify or dehumanize end-users or affected persons
applicable_requirements.action_needed: Implement opt-out mechanisms and de-identification protocols

applicable_requirements.requirement_text: AI system must not unduly or unfairly reduce psychological and emotional well-being of end-users and stakeholders
applicable_requirements.action_needed: Establish psychological impact monitoring during deployment

applicable_requirements.requirement_text: Consult local laws before collecting or analysing bulk surveillance data — surveillance must not predict protected categories or endanger individual well-being
applicable_requirements.action_needed: Verify compliance with Irish Data Protection Commission regulations for biometric data

applicable_requirements.requirement_text: Have you considered whether your NLP/ML system could out, profile or endanger vulnerable individuals?
applicable_requirements.action_needed: Evaluate potential for emotional profiling to harm student self-perception

applicable_requirements.requirement_text: Determine FRIA outcome status: Unacceptable Risk / High Residual Risk / Risks Acceptable / Risks Mitigated
applicable_requirements.action_needed: Classify system risk level based on adolescent vulnerability context

charter_rights_at_risk.relevance: Continuous biometric monitoring in classrooms infringes on private life
Minors cannot provide informed consent for surveillance participation
Collection of biometric data requires strict safeguards under GDPR
Emotional surveillance may objectify students as data points

historical_precedents.incident_title: Google Photos Labeled Black People as Gorillas
historical_precedents.lesson: Algorithmic bias in facial recognition systems can produce discriminatory outcomes
Surveillance in educational settings raises significant privacy and autonomy concerns

historical_precedents.incident_title: Amazon Alexa Recording Children Without Consent
historical_precedents.lesson: Voice data collection from minors requires explicit safeguards and opt-out options
Unvalidated AI systems can perpetuate systemic biases when applied to new populations

recommended_mitigations.mitigation: Implement opt-out mechanisms for individual students
Conduct bias audits across adolescent age groups and ethnicities
Establish independent ethics review board oversight
Provide real-time opt-in/opt-out controls during classroom sessions
Develop anonymization protocols for emotional data storage

tier1_mandatory_actions: Conduct Formal Risk Impact Assessment (FRIA) per EU AI Act requirements
Obtain explicit opt-out options for all students
Validate emotion recognition accuracy across adolescent demographics
Implement data minimization protocols for biometric storage
Provide detailed informed consent documentation for parents
tier2_reflective_prompts: How might continuous emotional monitoring affect student-teacher dynamics?
What long-term psychological impacts could arise from being classified as 'disengaged' by an algorithm?
Should teachers receive specialized training to interpret emotional data responsibly?
How can we balance educational insights with student privacy rights?
What safeguards are needed for aggregated data shared with third parties?
